sanskrit: महाभूतान्यहङ्कारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च । इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः ॥
hindiTranslation: मूल प्रकृति, समष्टि बुद्धि महत्तत्त्व, समष्टि अहंकार, पाँच महाभूत और दस इन्द्रियाँ, एक मन तथा पाँचों इन्द्रियोंके पाँच विषय   यह चौबीस तत्त्वोंवाला क्षेत्र है ॥
englishTranslation: The five great elements, ego, intellect, the unmanifest, the ten senses, the one mind, and the five sense-objects: this is the field in brief.
essence: What you call “me” is a changing system, not a single thing.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: The body-mind world is made of many parts: elements, ego, intellect, senses, mind, and sense-objects. Krishna is naming the field so Arjuna can see it clearly.
simpleInsight: You stop mistaking your changing equipment for who you are.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield, Arjuna is frozen, and Krishna begins mapping reality with precision. After introducing the field and the knower of the field, he lists the twenty-four components that make up the field itself, so Arjuna can separate what changes from what knows.
modernRelevance: You sit in a therapist’s office after saying, “I don’t know what’s wrong with me.” The first relief is naming the parts: body, thoughts, impulses, roles, reactions. Clarity starts there.
translationLiteral: महाभूतानि (the five great elements) / अहङ्कारः (ego) / बुद्धिः (intellect) / अव्यक्तम् (the unmanifest) / एव (indeed) / च (and) / इन्द्रियाणि (the senses) / दश (ten) / एकम् (one) / च (and) / पञ्च (five) / च (and) / इन्द्रियगोचराः (sense-objects)
insight: It trains the mind to dissect experience into components instead of collapsing everything into a single identity.
